Address: 98C9+73G, Lioka, Towuti, East Luwu Regency, South Sulawesi 92983
Description:
The rock art in Andomo Cave was first discovered during an archeology survey in 2011. Another research was conducted in 2013 to re-identify the rock art forms and to further study the variant
of rock art motifs, and to recapitulate the total number of the rock art images. In 2012, there were only three panels of rock art that could be identified (the information on the location of the hand stencils in picture 1), while in 2013, there were 5 panels that could be re-identified.
Based on the description of the types of the objects, the printed images were generally stencils of the hands, the feet, and other unrecognized stencils. There were 55 images found in Andomo Cave on
5 panels, which were spread all over the upper chamber. These images applied the engraving techniques and were commonly colored in red. The documentation of panel 1 up to panel 5 was sequenced starting from the most western part of the area to the east. The rock art images were positioned in an adequately sunlight-exposed area. There were 42 rock art images that applied the spray technique using red and black colors printed on the right and left wall of the western part of
the cave, but unfortunately most of the images have peeled off. The depicted motifs consisted of hand stencils (33 drawings), remnants of hand stencils (5 drawings), foot stencils (1 drawing), and unrecognized images (3 drawings) that were positioned approximately 1 meter to 1.5 meters from the cave floor.
Panel 1 has the largest number of images in Andomo Cave consisting of 13 hand stencils that were imprinted using the finger-point technique while the other 29 drawings applied the normal technique. A total of 23 images were identified
as using the fingers up to the hand palm ranging from five fingers to three fingers, and 13 images used only the finger-point technique. However, there were no hand stencil images that were drawn using the fingers up to the upper arms. The orientation of the hand stencils varied, with ten images that were oriented upwards, six images were oriented downward, nine images were printed facing the left, and twelve images were drawn facing the right. In addition, three images were identified as the left hand and there was one image depicting the right hand. In panel 2, two rock art images were imprinted, consisting of a foot stencil (?) and one unidentified image colored in red using the spray technique. Meanwhile, in panel 3, the rock art was drawn around 1.15 m above the cave floor, consisting of
5 images that were found with four hand stencil images but one image could not be identified, however all of them were colored in red using the spray technique. One hand stencil was printed using the finger-pointed style facing the left, showing the fingers up to the palm. In panel 3, the hand stencil showed an image of the wrist facing left, which was not found in panel 1. On panel 4, there were only remains of unidentified red shape images that were imprinted using the spray technique. As for panel 5, there were five red hand stencils that also used the spray technique, with three images only consisted of the fingers pointing upwards while the other two images showed fingers up to the palm pointing downwards. There was one image that was printed using the pointed finger style.
Author: Eka Permana

Address: Tira Wonua, Routa, Konawe Regency, South East Sulawesi
Description:
The site consists of a niche located on a limestone range which measures 21 meters wide, 5 meters high and around 8 meters inward. The findings from the cave floor consisted of human skulls and bone fragments, ceramic fragments from China and Europe, bronze earrings, and pottery pieces. Three green hand stencil images were found on the ceiling of the niche (LPA 2012)
Based on the survey report on 2012, the artifacts found in Lampetia Cave Site included porcelain shards, ceramics, human bones and beads. In 2012, an excavation was conducted to open two test pits (A and B) and during this excavation, the researchers also explored the possibility of identifying rock art in this site. The observation covered the entire cave wall and several rock
art images that consisted of hand stencils were found but the images had extremely worn-off. The imprinted rock art was located in a sun-exposed area.
The three hand stencil findings were located on the upper wall and the west side of the excavation pit. In identifying the rock art, the images were divided into two panels. Panel 1 consisted of one stencil that depicted four fingers up to the palm, but not decorated, and oriented upwards, colored greenish black and using the spray technique. The images were imprinted 2 meters from the cave floor. Panel 2 consisted of two hand stencils that were oriented upwards with the first image on top. The hand stencils consisted of three fingers oriented upwards that were still visible, and were colored greenish black using the spray technique.
Author: Oktaviana

Address: FHCC+WHV Sulaa, Bau-Bau City, South East Sulawesi

Site_Name: Het Sor Sorot Rock Art Site
Address: Wonreli, Pulau-pulau Terselatan, Maluku Barat Daya Regency, Maluku
Description: The surface of the niche wall is green mixed with black. The number of human images on the site Het Sor Sorot as many as fourteen with quite good condition with a variety of red and orange picture colours. The shape of anthropomorphic images as many as fourteen pieces that show the anatomy as complete humans, including having a body, both hands, both feet, and one head. The shape of the head is a long round oval shape with a kind of accessory on the top of the head with an elongated shape and part described as not having that accessory. Anthropomorphic is depicted with several poses, such as a standing expression, both legs stretched open parallel, and both hands raised up in an open position. In addition, there are depictions with dance expressions / party events with supporting accessories such as chairs. The condition of the image in certain parts is solidly concentrated and some are not.
Author: Wattimena

Address: Wonreli, Pulau-pulau Terselatan, Maluku Barat Daya Regency, Maluku
Description: The condition of the rock drawing is very good with a moveable position that is easy to achieve. The colours of anthropomorphic rock images on the Herku Site are light brown, yellow, and white. Anthropomorphic at the Herku Site is depicted on a slightly rough wall surface with a scale of 10 cm. The anthropomorphic motif describes human anatomy, namely the body, two legs, two hands, and one head. At the Herku Site, several forms of anthropomorphic images were found with a) perpendicular position, b) a perpendicular standing position with both hands raised facing up (letter U). and c) anthropomorphic motifs are depicted singular.
Author: Wattimena

Address: Wonreli, Pulau-pulau Terselatan, Maluku Barat Daya Regency, Maluku
Description: Only one anthropomorphic red figure
Author: Wattimena

Address: Wonreli, Pulau-pulau Terselatan, Maluku Barat Daya Regency, Maluku
Description: At the Irmula Site, there are nine anthropomorphic motifs located on the left and right sides of the Irmula Cave wall. The nine anthropomorphic motifs are described as having a complete human body anatomy, namely one head, body, two legs, two hands. In addition, it is depicted with the expression of both hands open upwards, the shape of a round head.
Author: Wattimena

Address: Wonreli, Pulau-pulau Terselatan, Maluku Barat Daya Regency, Maluku
Description: At the Kulwasuru Site, there are three anthropomorphic motifs in good condition and clearly visible in shape. The anthropomorphic motif is depicted in a perpendicular shape with both hands stretched out to below, while both legs are spread open. The anthropomorphic motif is depicted whole as having one head, two legs, body, and hands. The shape of the anthropomorphic head is elongated oval, and there are also round and triangular shapes. The colour of the anthropomorphic motif at the Kulwasuru Site is black and red.
Author: Wattimena

Address: Wonreli, Pulau-pulau Terselatan, Maluku Barat Daya Regency, Maluku
Description: At the Lenhorhorok Site, there are five anthropomorphic motifs that are described as having the complete anatomy of the human body, namely the head, feet, body, and hands. The colour of the picture is red and orange. The shape of the anthropomorphic motif is depicted perpendicular with both legs open in parallel. Both hands are stretched out and the head is perpendicular. The head is depicted in several forms, including somewhat elongated upwards and like using two horns. The expression of anthropomorphic motifs of the two hands above is open and parallel, and the other motifs of both hands are stretched parallel downwards.
Author: Wattimena

Address: Wonreli, Pulau-pulau Terselatan, Maluku Barat Daya Regency, Maluku
Description: On the Liotitin Site, there are eleven anthropomorphic motifs with red and orange colours. The eleven anthropomorphic motifs are described complete with the anatomical structure of the human body, including the head, body, two legs, and two hands.
Author: Wattimena

Address: Wonreli, Pulau-pulau Terselatan, Maluku Barat Daya Regency, Maluku
Description: This site has 23 anthropomorphic rock images. The colour of the anthropomorphic image at the Salpuru Site is red. The entire anthropomorphic motif has the anatomy of the human body, namely one head, body, two legs, and two hands. In addition, there is an anthropomorphic motif with both hands raised up, both legs open and there is an object at the waist, such as a machete or sword.
Author: Wattimena

Address: Lekloor, Pulau-pulau Terselatan, Maluku Barat Daya Regency, Maluku
Description: This site has ten anthropomorphic rock images. The shape of the anthropomorphic rock motif is depicted in an inverted position (depicted simply). There are not many anthropomorphic indentations. The anthropomorphics on this site are contextualised with other images, namely boats.
Author: Wattimena

Address: R2P8+GVC Kaimear, Tual City, Maluku
Description: The red anthropomorphic image is located on the niche wall of the Kel Lein Site. The condition of the anthropomorphic image is not maintained, Part of it has faded and erased. The average image size is 10 cm with a height from the surface of the niche floor below 5 metres. The image of the anthropomorphic rock at the Kel Lein Site gives certainty that the shape is a human with the expression standing upright with both legs open, and arms bent holding the waist. The image on the head is circular, but a bit narrow. His body is straight at the waist and something is depicted rather large. The position of the picture is like a soldier resting in place. The picture explains human activities related to the livelihood system. The colour of the red anthropomorphic motif with a scale size of 10 cm, is depicted on the surface of the uneven wavy niche wall. The surface of the niche wall is light brown.
Author: Wattimena

Address: Kiltai, Seram Timur, East Seram, Maluku
Description:
The motif displayed was dark red using ochre
material (hematite) using brush and spray techniques; the
type of painting is in the form of dots, lines, outlines,
solids, and negatives. The identified rock art specifically,
i.e., human figure, negative hand stencil, boat motif,
geometric motif, vertical stripe motif, fish motif, and
remnants of unidentified motifs. Many rock arts were
faded out; some were covered in moss, insect nests,
exposed to water flow, and flaking of the cliff wall. On
Watu Sika cliff, the remnants of insect nests were
yellowish red, and the natural rocks were red were due
to the natural process on the cliff wall which can interfere
rock art identification. However, as the materials used
for rock art on cliff wall were different, so the rock art
motif could be identified, and the remaining of the insect
nest and the red color of the rock could be distinguished.
Based on the results of the analysis it is known that
there are 66 rock art motifs on this site, consisting of
10.5% of figurative motifs, 25.7% of non-figurative
motifs, and the total of unidentified motifs were 63.8 %
of the remaining motifs. It used spray techniques of 45 motifs predominantly, brush techniques as many as 20
motifs, and combination of brush and spray technique
was 1 motif. The height of the wall panels of rock art
from the terrace surface of the cliff up to the ceiling
ranged from about 0.7 to 2.5 meters. Rock art panels on
the wall of Watu Sika Cliff could be seen by standing on
a boat or sitting on a cliff terrace to see the rock art motifs
at a close range.
Author: Oktaviana, Lape

Address: 9MJJ+934, Ohoidertawun, Kei Kecil, Sotheast Maluku Regency, Maluku
Description: The scribbles of pictures or paintings on the cliff walls describe many daily life and human religious activities in the past. There are pictures in the shape of humans, sun, flora, and fauna. There is a picture of a boat showing the life of people at that time have known the means of transportation, especially in water transportation. There are also hand-printed paintings.

Address: Buano Utara, Huamual Belakang, West Seram, Maluku
Description:
The rock art niche is located about 80
meters above sea level. This rock art niche is located on
a pretty high cliff wall, so it is relatively dangerous to
climb it. Rock climbing types of equipment are required
as an essential requirement of safety standards to reach
the rock art niche. The
characteristics of Austronesian rock art motifs are also
similarly evident in the Hatupatola site and the Tanjung
Bintang site, such as anthropomorphic, geometric, sun,
and circle motifs, as well as being in a difficult to reach
position, relatively high cliff face. Based on all the motifs
that can still be observed, most of the shapes are still
relatively looks clear, but some of the motifs appear to
have faded, and there are also star motifs that are almost
entirely eroded. In the sun-ray shape motif, apart from
having faded in some parts, there are also indications of
vandalism over the pigment in the core of the circle. One
of the unique features of the sun-ray motif rock art at the
Tanjung Bintang Site is the appearance of green
pigments. Based on recorded and confirmed
archaeological data so far, the green pigment in rock art
in Maluku has never been found before.
Author: Muhammad Al Mujabuddawat

Address: Wakapsir, Alor Barat Laut, Alor Regency, East Nusa Tenggara
Exposed Deposition: Flexed or Semi-Flexed Position,Seated or Squatted Position,Second Burial
Description:
Tron Bon Lei is comprised of two adjoining shelters located within a series of shelters in a vol-canic ridge which runs along the south coast of Alor, near Lerabain village.
Excavations in 2014 documented a large amount of vertebrate and invertebrate fauna, lithics, shell ornaments, and fish-hooks which occurred as grave goods. Human remains were documented in two of the test pits excavated in the two adjoining shelters in 2014. The cranial remains from square C (TLC-1) were dated using OSL to c.17kya BP. A complete chronometric sequence from the original excavations in Tron Bon Lei identi-fied four phases of human occupations: Late Holocene (c.2.7–5.4kya cal BP), middle Holocene(c.7.4–8.2kya cal BP), early Holocene-terminal Pleistocene (c.9.4–12.7kya cal BP), and LatePleistocene (c.17.4-21kya cal BP).
Commonalities between Mainland and Island Southeast Asian mortuary treatments begin-ning in the Late Pleistocene, with a common custom of flexed burial as seen in Tron Bon Lei(TLB-1), suggest transmission of ideas.

In Tron Bon Lei, rock art occurs in both sections of
the overhang (Tron Bon Lei 1 and 2) where the shelter
walls protect a flat floor deposit. As the
shelters are part of a continuous overhang they are discussed here simply as Tron Bon Lei. Anthropomorphs
are the most common figurative motifs found at Tron
Bon Lei followed by ‘boats’. 
Tron Bon Lei also contains red hand prints. Anthropomorphs are the most common figurative
motif at Tron Bon Lei with at least ten recorded. All
are in red pigment. The head in all figures is
shown in solid red pigment. Some figures appear to
be holding weapons. one wielding an ‘axe’ and another one has
a possible club and a ‘shield’. The anthropomorphs on
the left of the panel appear to be wearing
headdresses of a fan-like shape.
Author: Yuwono, O'Connor, Carro

Address: Wakapsir, Alor Barat Laut, Alor Regency, East Nusa Tenggara
Description:
Ba Lei is a complex of three small caves about 300 m to the west of Tron Bon Lei. Ba Lei 1, the largest of the three contained some faded patches of red pigment and several
simple white geometrics. Ba Lei 2
the second largest cave in the complex had no obvious rock art but this may be due to poor preservation of pigment on the wall surfaces. Ba Lei 3, the smallest of the three caves is only 2 m high and 4 m wide at its maximum dimension but contained white geometric motifs and some faded red pigment.
Author: Yuwono, O'Connor, Carro

Description:
Associated with deposition of fossils at Boa Lesa, Mata Menge, Kobatuwa, Ngamapa, Kopowatu and Pauphadhi between 840,000 and 700,000 years ago.
Fauna present at the time included large
Stegodon, crocodile and giant rat. In situ
stone artefacts occur at all these sites.

Address: QF5V+2FF, Bungamuda, Ile Ape, Lembata Regency, East Nusa Tenggara
Description:
Rock art at the Nali site was found on a large volcanic boulder in the
village of Atawtung-Lamagute, Lamalera district, on the northern slope

of the Ile Lewotolok volcano. The boulder belongs to a Holocene vol-
canic formation of the still-active volcano Ile Lewotolok (Koesoemadi-
nata and Noya, 1990). The rock art at the Nali site shows a standing

anthropomorphic figure comprising subimages of hands, fingers, feet,
and toes (Fig. 1b). O’Connor and coworkers (2018b) also identified
other subimages, such as male genitalia, a protrusion on both sides of the
head, and a head adornment at the center of the head.
Rock art at the Nali site possesses a distinctive characteristic that is
uncommon in the corpus of rock art in East Indonesia and East Timor;
this site presents a white-colored static-posed anthropomorphic figure

instead of an active-posed human figure drawn with red pigment (Bal-
lard, 1988; Ballard, 1992; O’Connor, 2003). Moreover, this rock art was

thought to show similarities to the bronze Dong Son drum (Moko drum)
image in an ancestral village in Lewohala, East Flores, East Nusa
Tenggara (O’Connor et al., 2018b).
Author: Moh. Mualliful Ilmi et. al

Address: QP56+3M3 Dolulolong, Lembata Regency, East Nusa Tenggara
Description:
The Tene Koro site is located in the Uaq Loroq area, Dolulolong,
Lembata, East Nusa Tenggara. The rock art is depicted on a boulder
made up of tuffaceous sandstones, which was from the upper Miocene
Waihekang Formation (Koesoemadinata and Noya, 1990). The rock art
image at the Tene Koro site depicts a boat equipped with an up-raked
Author: Moh. Mualliful Ilmi et. al

Address: Tepian Langsat, Bengalon, East Kutai Regency, East Kalimantan
Description:
The lithic studies for the sites of Liang Jon, Liang Abu and Liang
Pemalawan were conducted by three different archaeologists from
2003 (Liang Jon: J. Espagne in 2003 and 2005, M. Grenet in 2007,
2008, 2009 and 2014; Liang Abu: J. Sarel in 2009 and 2012, M.
Grenet and R. Fauzi in 2013 and 2014; Liang Pemalawan: M. Grenet
and R. Fauzi in 2013). In 2003 two test pits (S1 and S2) were dug. These excavations
delivered many remains from the lithic industry, earthenware
potsherds (at the top of the stratigraphic sequence) and faunal
remains including many terrestrial gastropod shells. In S2, two fire
structures superimposed on each other were uncovered.
The investigation of the site was completed during the 2005
fieldwork campaign by the excavation of two additional test pits on
the surface: SA and SB (Chazine and Ferrie, 2008  ). SA stopped at a
depth of 0.60 m as a complete extended burial was found. SB was

dug to the depth of 3 m; the bedrock was not reached. Many un-
defined artifacts were found. At the depth of 2.45/2.60 m datation

gave 10,260 ± 60 BP (SacA 13859, 14C AMS-charcoal, Gay, 2010).
In 2007 two test pit trenches were excavated following the main
axis of the shelter: TA in the southern part of the shelter and TB

along the eastern wall. A third trench TC was excavated orthogo-
nally (EW) to the axis of TA and TB, joining respectively their

southern and northern ends. All studied artifacts come from these
three trenches.
In 2008, an area of 8 m2 was delimited. It was located in the
middle part of the shelter (see Fig. 4 under). It was only conducted
at a depth of 25 cm. This excavation provided essentially faunal
remains, vegetal remains, beads, some bone industry but no lithic
artifacts since the settlement was too recent. A broken skull found in situ, fully ochre-tinted, was found in the southern part. The datation of a charcoal is very close to that of TC: 4035 ± 30 BP. (SacA

19314, 14C AMS for charcoal, Gay, 2010). This finding of an ochre-
tinted skull is comparable to the Mesolithic burial practices

described in Niah (Barker et al., 2011) and might be, even if in a
younger context, culturally linked to the neighboring burial (SA test
pit) excavated in 2005 and dated to around 5600 BP, i.e.
5539 ± 33 BP (UBA-19256, AMS bioapatite for bone) and
5634 ± 35 BP (UBA-19257, AMS bioapatite for a tooth).
Author: Grenet, Sarel, Oktaviana, Chazine

Address: Merabu, Kelay, Berau Regency, East Kalimantan
Description:
Remains of relatively
recent funerary practices (human bones, wood and rattan remains,
and few pottery sherds) have been found in these upper cavities. The lithic artifact assemblages collected in Liang Abu showed a

high typo-technological similarity with that gathered on the sur-
face at Liang Pemalawan (and other neighboring sites in the Lesan

River basin like Liang Wanat, Liang Ara, Liang Beloyot Atas) during
the 2010 survey. These similar debitages are related to knappers
aiming to produce a group of particular products.
Author: Grenet, Sarel, Oktaviana, Chazine

Address: Mapulu, Kelay, Berau Regency, East Kalimantan
Description:
One date was obtained for Liang Pemalawan from a charcoal
sample removed in TP2 at the base of a small fire structure in
contact with SU3. The date obtained is 720 BP (BETA 353017, 14C
AMS e charcoal). This date seems very recent compared with the

similar earthenware sample of Liang Abu and with the dates ob-
tained for SU2 in Liang Abu (UBA-20839: 1672 ± 21 BP and UBA-
20840: 1524 ± 22 BP).
Author: Grenet, Sarel, Oktaviana, Chazine

Address: Tepian Langsat, Bengalon, East Kutai Regency, East Kalimantan
Description:
Karim cave was one of the prehistoric caves discovered in the area
of Marang Mountain, Sangkulirang–Mangkalihat karst region in 2001
[11]. Karim Cave is 208 m above sea level with a cave mouth size of
45 m, the hall width of 19 m, and a total area of 582,15 m2
. Karim Cave
contained various type of rock art figures, including the 12 negative
hand stencils, a beehive-like image with a height of 1.3 m and a width
of up to 1 m, and a dark red animal with a length of 1 m and a height of
55 cm [11]. The example of the animal was the species called Tapirus
indicus, which had been extinct in Kalimantan Island since ca. 6000
years ago. The physicochemical studies of pigment samples showed that he-
matite was the source of material pigment, whereas the purple pig-
ments showed the highest hematite content (29.9%) and the highest

hematite phase crystallinity compared to dark-red and red pigments.
Author: Ilmi, Nurdini, Maryanti

Address: Gunung Piring, Mertak, Pujut, Central Lombok Regency, West Nusa Tenggara
Description:
Based  on  several  archaeological  finds  which 
were observed on the surface soil in earlier research, 
the  location  where  the  actual  excavation  should 
take  place  was  determined.  The  area  was  divided 
in  two  parts,  Sector  I  and  Sector  II.  Sector  I 
covered  the  level  part  on  the  top  of  the  hill, 
while  Sector  II  was  located  on  a  lower  part,  about 
ten  meters  further  down.  With  the  difference 
in  height,  it  was  hoped  that  finds  characteristic 
of different levels of erosion could be observed.
Sector  I  measured  73.5  x  42  meters  and 
thirteen  boxes  have  been  dug;  Sector  II  measured 
12 x 7.5 meters and two boxes have been dug.
A    human    skeleton    was    encountered    in    ol7' 
and pl7' of Sector I along with fragments of bone and  teeth  in  several  other  boxes  which  indicated 
the  existence  of  burial  places.  One  method  of 
burial  is  in  the  recumbent  posture  with  head 
towards  the  west  and  accompanied  by  burial 
goods  in  the  form  of  decorated  and  smooth 
pottery.
Archaeological  finds  gathered  from  both 
the  surface  and  the  excavation  show  that  at  one 
time  the  Gunung  Piring  site  was  a  burial  ground 
and  corresponds  to  the  bronze-iron  age.  There 
were  also  indications  that  later  on  at  one  time 
this  site  was  again  the  habitation  of  people,  judged 
from the finds of ceramics.
Thus  the  Gunung  Piring  site  can  offer  aspects 
of  life  of  the  past,  primarily  with  respect  to  the 
burial methods used by the people of this area.
In  addition  to  the  archaeological  observations 
made  from  the  surface  survey  as  well  as  the 
excavation,  the  research  also  included  observation 
on  geological  symtoms,  comprising  morphology, 
litology and hydrology.
Biblio_ref: Laporan ekskavasi Gunung Piring 1976 : Lombok Tengah-Selatan / penyusun laporan Goenadi Nitihaminoto
Author: Nitihaminoto, Simandjuntak, Suwarno
